title: Fix CSS Flexbox Not Centering Vertically (Complete Guide)
excerpt: Flexbox centering not working vertically? Learn the #1 cause and 3 proven solutions that work every time.
content:
Fix CSS Flexbox Not Centering Vertically (Complete Guide)

You're using align-items: center but your flex items still hug the top of the container. This is the #1 Flexbox centering mistake every developer makes - and it's incredibly easy to fix.

The Problem

Expected: Perfect center alignment (both horizontally and vertically).

Reality: Items center horizontally but stick to the top vertically.

This happens on hero sections, modals, cards - anywhere you want true centering.

The Cause

Flexbox needs height to center vertically.

Without defined height, the container collapses to content height, so there's no room to center. align-items: center works horizontally because flex-direction is row by default.

Solution 1: Viewport Height (Most Common)

.hero {
  display: flex;
  align-items: center;
  justify-content: center;
  min-height: 100vh;
}

Solution 2: Parent Height

.container {
  display: flex;
  align-items: center;
  height: 400px; /* or whatever height */
}

Solution 3: Flex Grow (Modern Approach)

.wrapper {
  display: flex;
  flex-direction: column;
  min-height: 100vh;
}
.content {
  margin: auto;
}

Result

✅ Perfect centering every time
✅ Works with any content height
✅ Responsive and flexible

Bonus Tips

  • Always test with dynamic content - fixed heights fail here
  • Use min-height instead of height when possible
  • Combine with padding for breathing room: padding: 2rem

Conclusion

The fix? Give your flex container height using min-height: 100vh. That's it. This single line solves 95% of vertical centering issues.

Pro tip: Bookmark this - you'll need it weekly.

title: JavaScript Event Listener Not Working After DOM Update (Event Delegation Fix)
excerpt: Added elements via JS? Event listeners broken? Use event delegation - the solution that works with dynamic content forever.
content:
JavaScript Event Listener Not Working After DOM Update (Event Delegation Fix)

You add a 'click' listener to buttons. Then dynamically create more buttons. Old buttons work, new ones don't. Classic problem with a simple fix.

The Problem

Works fine initially:

document.querySelectorAll('.btn').forEach(btn =>
  btn.addEventListener('click', handleClick)
);

Then you add new buttons with JS. New buttons do nothing when clicked.

The Cause

Event listeners only attach to EXISTING elements. When you use querySelectorAll, it finds elements at that moment. New elements get created after, so no listeners attached.

Solution: Event Delegation

Listen on a parent element that exists forever:

document.addEventListener('click', function(e) {
  if (e.target.matches('.btn')) {
    handleClick(e);
  }
});

Advanced: Classes & Data Attributes

document.addEventListener('click', function(e) {
  if (e.target.matches('[data-action="delete"]')) {
    const id = e.target.dataset.id;
    deleteItem(id);
  }
});

Result

✅ Works with ALL buttons (old + new)
✅ Better performance (1 listener vs many)
✅ Handles dynamically loaded content

Bonus Tips

  • Use matches() - faster than classList.contains
  • Stop propagation: e.stopPropagation() for nested elements
  • Event delegation = future-proof your JS

Conclusion

Stop adding individual listeners. Use event delegation on document.body. One line of code handles infinite dynamic elements.

title: Responsive Images Without Media Queries (srcset + sizes)
excerpt: Slow mobile sites from huge images? Native responsive images serve perfect size automatically - no CSS needed.
content:
Responsive Images Without Media Queries (srcset + sizes)

Your hero image loads lightning-fast on desktop but kills mobile performance. Modern HTML fixes this without a single media query.

The Problem

Desktop (1920px image): Loads instantly

Mobile (414px screen): Downloads 1920px image, scales down = waste

The Cause

Browsers download regardless of screen size. No smarts built in.

Solution: srcset + sizes

How It Works

  • srcset = available sizes
  • sizes = "how big will it display?"
  • Browser picks perfect match

Result

✅ Mobile: ~384px image (fast)
✅ Desktop: ~1536px (sharp)
✅ 60-80% smaller payloads

Bonus Tips

  • Convert to WebP: hero-768.webp 768w
  • Art direction: for crop ratios
  • Test with Lighthouse - instant score boost

Conclusion

Drop srcset + sizes on your images. Watch Core Web Vitals jump. No JavaScript. No CSS. Pure HTML magic.

title: CSS Grid Items Overflowing Container (3 Quick Fixes)
excerpt: Grid items breaking layout? Long text/content pushing outside container? 3 bulletproof solutions.
content:
CSS Grid Items Overflowing Container (3 Quick Fixes)

Your CSS Grid looks perfect... until someone adds long text or tall images. Then items explode outside the container. Here's the fix.

The Problem

Grid items with long unbroken content (URLs, long words) or tall images ignore container boundaries.

The Cause

Grid by default doesn't constrain item content. Long words/images create implicit tracks larger than container.

Solution 1: Container Overflow

.grid {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(300px, 1fr));
  gap: 2rem;
  overflow: hidden;
}

Solution 2: Item Constraints

.grid-item {
  overflow: hidden;
  word-break: break-word;
  hyphens: auto;
}

Solution 3: minmax + clamp (Best)

.grid {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, 
    minmax(clamp(250px, 25vw, 350px), 1fr));
  gap: clamp(1rem, 3vw, 2rem);
}

Result

✅ Bulletproof responsive grid
✅ Handles any content length
✅ No horizontal scrollbars

Bonus Tips

  • Images: object-fit: cover; height: 200px;
  • Long URLs: overflow-wrap: anywhere;
  • Mobile first: start with 1fr, add columns later

Conclusion

overflow: hidden on container + minmax() columns = unbreakable grids. Copy, paste, done.

title: Prevent Form Double Submission (Bulletproof Solution)
excerpt: Users double-clicking submit? Duplicate orders/database entries? One simple technique stops it forever.
content:
Prevent Form Double Submission (Bulletproof Solution)

Users panic-click submit button = duplicate orders, double charges, spam database entries. This stops it instantly.

The Problem

Slow server response + anxious users = multiple identical submissions.

The Cause

No submit button state management. Button stays clickable during form processing.

Solution: Triple Protection

const form = document.querySelector('#my-form');
const submitBtn = document.querySelector('#submit-btn');
let isSubmitting = false;

form.addEventListener('submit', async function(e) {
  if (isSubmitting) {
    e.preventDefault();
    return;
  }
  
  isSubmitting = true;
  submitBtn.disabled = true;
  submitBtn.textContent = 'Processing...';
  
  try {
    await submitForm(form);
  } finally {
    isSubmitting = false;
    submitBtn.disabled = false;
    submitBtn.textContent = 'Send Message';
  }
});

Simple Version (Non-Async)

form.addEventListener('submit', function(e) {
  if (submitBtn.disabled) {
    e.preventDefault();
    return;
  }
  submitBtn.disabled = true;
  submitBtn.innerHTML = 'Sending...';
});

Result

✅ Zero duplicate submissions
✅ Clear user feedback
✅ Handles slow networks

Bonus Tips

  • Visual feedback: spinner + text change
  • Timeout reset: re-enable after 30s max
  • Multiple forms? Use closest('form')

Conclusion

Disable button + change text + preventDefault = perfect form protection. Copy this pattern everywhere.
